briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the climate briefing for May 23, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensification of the Climate Crisis and Urgent Warnings
sections.content: The threat of climate change is becoming a reality, demanding immediate global action. Domestically, summers have lengthened by four weeks, and the number of heatwave days has surged [5]. Ecosystem changes are also being detected, such as plants at the peak of Seoraksan responding most rapidly to climate change [3]. Particularly alarming research indicates that greenhouse gas emissions from rice paddies, especially methane, have doubled over the past 60 years, underscoring the severe climate impact of the agricultural sector [9]. Internationally, anoxia in lakes worldwide is projected to worsen due to climate change [33], and the likelihood of deadly heatwaves in India and Pakistan has been analyzed to have tripled [43]. The UK Climate Change Committee warned that the country is 'built for a climate that no longer exists,' citing instances of widespread anxiety due to coastal erosion [36, 48].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Climate Action and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: In response to the climate crisis, various adaptation and mitigation efforts are actively underway both domestically and internationally. In the agricultural sector, there have been technological advancements for stable production, such as the Rural Development Administration releasing a 'Climate Change Response Rice Cultivation Suitability Map' and successfully developing drought-resistant wheat varieties [2, 10]. The movement towards carbon neutrality is also spreading. Korea's first carbon-neutral campsite has been established to promote eco-friendly camping [11, 13, 14], and Gwangju City reduced greenhouse gas emissions by 310,000 tons last year, achieving a 94% completion rate for its carbon neutrality projects [16, 23]. Furthermore, the Korea Chamber of Commerce and Industry and Korea Southern Power signed an MOU to implement carbon neutrality and promote energy transition [19, 21], while Gwangmyeong City is preparing a recruitment and support program for households installing renewable energy [26, 29]. In Jinan County, efforts to build a renewable energy self-sufficient economy through 'Sunshine Income Villages' are gaining attention [30, 31], and discussions continue regarding whether artificial intelligence can help solve the climate crisis [41]. Internationally, the COP30 presidency made significant progress by securing support for a proposal to halt deforestation at the UN Forum on Forests [47].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Science-Based Policy Formulation and Energy Transition Discussions
sections.content: Domestically, there are active discussions and efforts to establish a scientific foundation for responding to climate change. The Presidential Committee on Climate Change Response is playing a crucial role by determining the direction for drafting scientific reports that will guide global climate change responses [1, 15]. CMIP7, the next-generation climate science model, is enhancing the precision of climate predictions by presenting seven future scenarios [4], and at the 2026 Korean Society of Energy and Climate Change Spring Conference, technological innovation for achieving the 2035 NDC was emphasized [7]. Regarding energy transition, the RPS (Renewable Portfolio Standard) will be abolished next year, and the renewable energy market is set to be fully reorganized into a 'long-term auction system' [27]. However, the reintroduction of solar panel setback distance standards in the 'Renewable Energy Master Plan' has sparked controversy over deregulation [28]. Concurrently, a forum on sustainable carbon-neutral fuel technologies and policies was held [25], and the acceptance of carbon neutrality by Seoul mayoral candidates is also drawing attention [24], indicating ongoing discussions about carbon neutrality across society.

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Response and Challenges
sections.content: The international community continues legal and political discussions for climate change response. The UN General Assembly adopted a resolution affirming states' obligations regarding climate change response, emphasizing international responsibility [34]. The International Court of Justice (ICJ)'s advisory opinion on climate change is expected to set new standards for interpreting the Convention on Biological Diversity and international environmental law [6]. Meanwhile, diverse political stances and debates on climate change persist. While conservatives urged the Supreme Court to dismiss lawsuits related to climate change damages [38], a particular energy minister caused controversy by downplaying the importance of global warming [39, 45]. Former President Donald Trump claimed certain climate predictions were 'wrong,' but contrasting analyses also suggested that climate actions are showing effectiveness [42]. Amidst these developments, Singapore is exploring the next steps in climate action, aiming to set an example for the international community [44].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has once again highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, while also demonstrating the importance of diverse response efforts and policy discussions underway both domestically and internationally. We must strengthen climate resilience through continuous scientific approaches and society-wide cooperation. This concludes the climate briefing for May 23, 2026.

articles.original_title: ‘There is no great master plan’: anxiety as UK homes, roads and railways sink into the sea
articles.description: Increasing coastal erosion has hit communities’ livelihoods and put lifestyles under threatThe remains of the road linking two towns in south Devon lie crumbled on the foreshore in a mess of tarmac, steel and concrete.The dramatic coastal road, known as the Slapton Line, has an environmentally protected freshwater lake on one side and the sea on the other, and links the towns of Kingsbridge and Dartmouth. But this year, winter storms demolished a section of the A road between Torcross and Slapto

summary.phenomenon: Increased coastal erosion in South Devon, UK, is threatening community livelihoods and lifestyles. This year, winter storms destroyed a section of the A road (Slapton Line) connecting Kingsbridge and Dartmouth, leaving debris crumbled on the foreshore.
summary.cause: Increased coastal erosion and winter storms have been identified as the causes of the road destruction.
summary.outlook: The increasing coastal erosion poses an ongoing threat to community livelihoods and lifestyles, with a lack of a clear master plan contributing to widespread anxiety.

articles.original_description: Increasing coastal erosion has hit communities’ livelihoods and put lifestyles under threatThe remains of the road linking two towns in south Devon lie crumbled on the foreshore in a mess of tarmac, steel and concrete.The dramatic coastal road, known as the Slapton Line, has an environmentally protected freshwater lake on one side and the sea on the other, and links the towns of Kingsbridge and Dartmouth. But this year, winter storms demolished a section of the A road between Torcross and Slapto
